reason: Response A reads as genuine Sonnet: it commits to a clear thesis (flawlessness/the immaculate), develops it through concrete sensory images (cracked glaze, weathered building, roughness in a voice), and uses the characteristic first-person reflective hedging ("I have come to think," "I suspect") that builds an argument rather than cataloging points. Response B, while fluent, has a more listy, explanatory, almost essay-template structure ("This is the beauty of...", "Compare that to...", "The overrated kind is also...") and leans on meta-commentary about social validation, which reads like a model competently imitating thoughtfulness rather than the more organically associative, image-driven voice Sonnet tends to produce. The slightly mechanical paragraph scaffolding and the tidy summarizing final line in B tip it toward the imitator.
